author: sanjaiyan-dev

body:
> And one doubt, `.hydrateRoot` called only once nah?

The original code works like this:

- Is variable `reactRoot` defined as a `ReactDOMClient.Root`?
  - No, `reactRoot` is null: Call `ReactDOMClient.hydrateRoot` to initiate it.
  - Yes, `reactRoot` is not null: Simply call `ReactDOMClient.Root.render` to update the existing React nodes.

That's why there is an `if (!reactRoot)` and a `reactRoot.render` in `client/app-index.tsx`. It is to make sure that no matter how many times `reactRenderElements` itself is being called, the method is still bullet-proof and no race condition.

But now you just break it. `Returntype<typeof requestIdleCallback>` is `number`, not `ReactDOMClient.Root`.

So let's say, `reactRenderElements` itself is being called twice.

- First time `reactRenderElements` is being called, `reactRoot` is `null`.
- `reactRoot = requestIdleCallback(() => { /** */ })`, now `reactRoot` is a `number`.
- The second time `reactRenderElements` is being called, `reactRoot` is not `null`
- `reactRenderElements` will execute `reactRoot.render`
- Now it throws an error: `Uncaught TypeError: <number>.render is not a function`
author: SukkaW
sanjaiyan-dev
SukkaW

body:
> > And one doubt, `.hydrateRoot` called only once nah?
> 
> The original code works like this:
> 
> * Is variable `reactRoot` defined as a `ReactDOMClient.Root`?
>   
>   * No, `reactRoot` is null: Call `ReactDOMClient.hydrateRoot` to initiate it.
>   * Yes, `reactRoot` is not null: Simply call `ReactDOMClient.Root.render` to update the existing React nodes.
> 
> That's why there is an `if (!reactRoot)` and a `reactRoot.render` in `client/app-index.tsx`. It is to make sure that no matter how many times `reactRenderElements` itself is being called, the method is still bullet-proof and no race condition.
> 
> But now you just break it. `Returntype<typeof requestIdleCallback>` is `number`, not `ReactDOMClient.Root`.
> 
> So let's say, `reactRenderElements` itself is being called twice.
> 
> * First time `reactRenderElements` is being called, `reactRoot` is `null`.
> * `reactRoot = requestIdleCallback(() => { /** */ })`, now `reactRoot` is a `number`.
> * The second time `reactRenderElements` is being called, `reactRoot` is not `null`
> * `reactRenderElements` will execute `reactRoot.render`
> * Now it throws an error: `Uncaught TypeError: <number>.render is not a function`

Can't we assign reactRoot inside `startTransition` ? 
```js
 requestIdleCallback(() => {
      (React as any)?.startTransition?.(() => {
        reactRoot  = (ReactDOMClient as any).hydrateRoot(domEl, reactEl)
      })
    })
```
author: sanjaiyan-dev

body:
> > Thanks a lot for explaining 😊
> > So, can we wrap `.render` with `startTransition` ? or can we do like this inside `app-next.js` ?
> 
> Or, `requestIdleCallback + startTransition` could be added inside `export function hydrate` just like this:
> 
> ```ts
> let hydrateIdleCallbackHandle = null | number;
> 
> export function hydrate(opts?: { onFlightCssLoaded?: () => Promise<void> }) {
>   if (hydrateIdleCallbackHandle) clearIdleCallback(hydrateIdleCallbackHandle);
>   hydrateIdleCallbackHandle = requestIdleCallback(() => {
>     startTransition(() => renderReactElement(appElement!, () => (
>       <React.StrictMode>
>         <Root>
>           <RSCComponent onFlightCssLoaded={opts?.onFlightCssLoaded} />
>         </Root>
>       </React.StrictMode>
>     )))
>   });
> }
> ```
> 
> IMHO, deferred hydration to reduce FID (First Input Delay) is a great idea, but where and how to add this is still depends on how Next.js Layout RFC is implemented.

🤩 So , do I need to change the code to like above (your one)  or close this PR ?

body: As mentioned in Slack, what do you think about making this a `console.warn()` and then returning `{}` in case the module is imported at the top-level but not actually leveraged?
author: ijjk

body:
I've tried various things, and discussed it with the edge team.

We believe the current behavior is right: attempt to access a property, function or constructor of an imported node.js module should throw.

Returning an empty object would confuse users because the thrown error is too generic:
```js
import os from 'os'
os.homedir === undefined
os.homedir() // throws TypeError "os.homedir is not a function"
```
Knowing whether "it's not actually leveraged" can not be done at runtime with an empty object. It requires static parsing and instrumentation, which prove to be unreliable in the past, and lead to this PR to change the behavior.

Now, the good news for Vercel Front is that, by simply bumping `@microlink/mql` to the latest and keep using its lightweight version, it won't use clean-stack and the error will be gone! CC @Kikobeats.

body:
So, there's one specific case that's the problem, where the user uses the `fill` mode on the image, but does not include height or width styling on the container element. In this case, the container element takes the intrinsic size of the image, even though `object-fit: "contain"` is set. This will cause layout shift.

This doesn't happen if the `img` element has `position: "absolute"` and the container element has `position: "relative"`. I haven't found any other techniques for keeping the unsized container from taking the size of the image.
author: atcastle

body: Does this really need to be an error? It doesn't hurt, does it? Imagine you have an abstraction that gives you image props and it always returns width and height. What is the harm?

Technically more correct is that the parent must have a position value that isn't static, right? E.g. `absolute` or `fixed` also work.

Is it good enough to enforce this for the immediate parent? I guess it is less of a foot-gun that way.

If the calculated ratio is 40%, I could have it say `You could try 'sizes="40vw"'` or something, but that seems pretty fragile, since it's not clear if that image will always be 40% of the screen, or it'll change a lot at different screen sizes (in which case a more complex sizes prop is required)

body:
My take is that if someone is adding `height` and `width` to an image in `fill` mode, 90%+ of the time it's going to be because they are making an error--they think that it will do something, and they're going to be confused about why it isn't having any effect. 

I'd think the case of having your own wrapper automatically providing height and width probably wouldn't need `fill` mode to begin with, or if it did the abstraction could be modified to provide `height/width` or `fill` but not both.
author: atcastle

body:
Yeah, it's going to be very easy to break the fill mode no-layout-shift guarantee by targeting the image with CSS, and I don't think there's anything we can really do about it. We COULD do a runtime check on the image's computed CSS position attribute, but I'm not sure we want to do that, as there are some cases where the image doesn't need position:absolute (such as if the containing element has an assigned height and width) so it wouldn't technically be wrong to change it.

I'm kind of on the fence about whether we should keep this check here, since it is one place where we have visibility into how the user is applying styles to the image, and we could at least warn them off from making certain basic mistakes.
author: atcastle

body:
The Next.js and React docs don't appear to recommend a location for type definition files. But I think the Contribution docs should recommend a directory for consistency. There are currently 13 examples that include type definition files differently:

- `interfaces/*.ts` 6 examples (including `with-typescript`, mostly from me copying this pattern, but not all types are interfaces)
- `types/*.ts`: 1 example (including `with-typescript-types`)
- `*.d.ts`: 3 examples (e.g. `environment.d.ts` for env variables, and other globals like `amp.d.ts`)
- `components/types.ts`: 1 example (co-located with components?)
- `lib/types.ts`: 1 example 
- `@types/*.ts`: 1 example

My preference is `types/*.ts` for page components and API Routes, and `*.d.ts` for globals.

body:
IMHO, adding `memo` to those components is unnecessary. Instead, you should consider `next/*` components as the fundamental building blocks of any Next.js project.

For instance, the `next/image` component can be used in `<Avatar />`, in `<Posts />`, in wherever you want. Thus the `next/image` prop is constantly changing, effectively making the `memo()` completely useless.

As for `next/head`, the component is dependent on its `children` prop (Yes, it is hard for new React developers to realize that the `children` is actually a "render prop"), and every time the parent of `next/head` updates, `next/head` will receive a fresh collection `children` (a collection of newly created JavaScript Object returned by either `React.createElement()` or React new JSX transform `_jsx()`), unless you have wrapped the `children` of `next/head` in `useMemo`. So in most cases, memoing `next/head` is also completely useless.

If you really believe the performance bottleneck of your project is `next/*` components, wrap their parent components (`<Avatar />`, `<Post />`, etc.) in the `memo()` instead. React reconcile will ignore diff for any children of a memoized component.

body:
**question(blocking):** should we alter route's source first, and then call `checkCustomRoutes()`?.

`checkCustomRoutes()` will call `tryToParsePath()` [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/5004a5a53a59e171dea9256751bace7d52655db2/packages/next/lib/load-custom-routes.ts#L276) and handles cases where the source could not be parsed, or is too big.

With the PR code, we're checking first, then adding to the source(s), and checking once more.
It creates a possibility where we'll overtake the 4096 character lenght (by adding data/basepath/i18n stuff).
Would it makes sense to process the sources, and then to invoke checkCustomRoutes at the end?

[tryToParsePath()](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/try-to-parse-path.ts#L21-L42) returns either an object with an error, either an object with `regexStr`. And since error have been handled by `checkCustomRoutes()`, we can't enter this conditional loop.

It's a bummer that `checkCustomRoutes()` does not exposes `regexStr`, it would save use from parsing the sources once more.

author: styfle

body:
```suggestion
First, the value of `sizes` is used by the browser to determine which size of the image to download, from `next/future/image`'s automatically-generated source set. When the browser chooses, it does not yet know the size of the image on the page, so it selects an image that is the same size or larger than the viewport. The `sizes` property allows you to tell the browser that the image will actually be smaller than full screen. If you don't specify a `sizes` value in an image with the `fill` property, a default value of `100vw` (full screen width) is used.
```
author: styfle

The `sizes` property allows you to tell the browser that the image will actually be smaller than full screen. If you don't specify a `sizes` value, a default value of `100vw` (full screen width) is used.

body:
Summary of the issue:
1. Hidden iframes are not guaranteed to ever run callbacks schedule through `requestAnimationFrame`. [This table](https://www.vector-logic.com/blog/posts/on-request-animation-frame-and-embedded-iframes#browser_variations) breaks it down browser to browser. For example, on Chrome, `window.requestAnimationFrame` is available, but the callback is never called in a hidden cross-origin iframe.
2. `displayContent` (below) is passed as an option to `beforeRender` which is [specifically `await`-ed](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0f99768f9811fdf027a083471bd2a435f6207f4a/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L436) before hydrating the app. If `displayContent` never resolves, the app **never** hydrates.
author: timothympace

body:
@magic-akari  Is it possible to preserve the original specifier?

E.g.:

```ts
const componentRoot = '@/some-components'
const Component1 = dynamic(() => import(`${componentRoot}/component1`));
const Component2 = dynamic(() => import(`${componentRoot}/component2`));
```

.. should turn into:

```ts
const componentRoot = '@/some-components'
[require.resolveWeak(`${componentRoot}/component1`]
[require.resolveWeak(`${componentRoot}/component2`]
```

It is up to Webpack to handle, evaluate and resolve `require.resolveWeak`, so we could leave the entire `TemplateLiteral` as-is (instead of asserting the `TemplateLiteral` only contains quasis).

body:
```suggestion
This example also includes hot-reloading when one of the `reducers` has changed. However, there is one caveat with this implementation: If you're using the `Redux DevTools` browser extension, then all previously recorded actions will be recreated when a reducer has changed (in other words, if you increment the counter by 1 using the `+1` button, and then change the increment action to add 10 in the reducer, Redux DevTools will playback all actions and adjust the counter state by 10 to reflect the reducer change). Therefore, to avoid this issue, the store has been set up to reset back initial state upon a reducer change. If you wish to persist redux state regardless (or you don't have the extension installed), then in `store.js` change (line 10) `createStore(reducers,initialState,composeWithDevTools(applyMiddleware(thunkMiddleware)))` to `createStore(reducers,initialState,applyMiddleware(thunkMiddleware))`.
```

body:
IMHO implementing an HTTP cache on Node.js from scratch is really, really, really a bad idea. Too many edge cases to handle (E.g., `cache: 'only-if-cached'` should return `504 Gateway Timeout` when cache missed), too many RFCs and specs to follow, and it is very difficult to truly mimic the browser's cache behavior.

If an HTTP cache is a must-have, use the existing library like [http-cache-semantics](https://www.npmjs.com/package/http-cache-semantics).

> Also notice that the `http-cache-semantics` doesn't support the `Headers` object. You need to transform the `Headers` object into plain object before passing to `http-cache-semantics`

Here is an example: package [make-fetch-happen](https://github.com/npm/make-fetch-happen), made and used by npm, which adds cache support to fetch (built on top of `http-cache-semantics`).
And another example: package [@adobe/helix-fetch](https://github.com/adobe/helix-fetch), which also implements cache support (which is also built on top of `http-cache-semantics`).

body:
`onLoadingComplete` does a few things:

- ensures the image is fully decoded 
- removes blur placeholder (not always necessary when svg background used but still necessary for css filter)
- guards against the react hydration issue since it runs even when `onLoad` was never called
author: styfle

body: does it run `onLoad` though? if the user provides a custom onLoad and onError to every img but only the onErrors get called for errors the symmetry is still off?
author: gnoff

body:
No the user must provide [`onLoadingComplete`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#onloadingcomplete).

We have always discouraged `onLoad` usage in both `next/image` and `next/future/image` for the 3 reasons above.

If we can solve all the corner cases for svg placeholder, we can probably start supporting `onLoad` in the future.

body: Thoughts on this?
author: leerob

body:
> Thoughts on this?

Correct me if I was wrong, but the new Layout RFC mentions that only `.tsx`, `.ts`, `.jsx` and `.js` are supported by default. So I still have to configure `pageExtention` if I want to use `page.mdx` or `layout.mdx`. The new layout and routing only supersedes `pageExtension` when putting non-page files (components, styles written in css-in-js, etc.) and page files together.

So IMHO, mentioning the new Layout RFC here could cause confusion. Even if the doc needs to mention it, it should be moved down to the "Including non-page files in the `pages` directory" parts.

body:
```suggestion
The `alt` property is used to describe the image for screen readers and search engines. It is also the fallback text if images have been disabled or an error occurs while loading the image.

It should contain replacement text that could be used instead of the image. It is not meant to supplement the image and should not repeat information that is already provided in the captions above or below the image.

If the image is decorative and doesn't present any information, the `alt` property should be an empty string (`alt=""`). See [A purely decorative image that doesn't add any information - HTML5 Spec](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#a-purely-decorative-image-that-doesn't-add-any-information)
```

https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/images.html#a-purely-decorative-image-that-doesn't-add-any-information

Add more information about why `alt` is a must-have (even if developers believe it could be omitted).

body:
Per React documents:

> `React.startTransition` lets you mark updates inside the provided callback as transitions.

However, calling `loadScript` will not trigger an "update" (`loadScript` will not cause a state update or trigger a re-render, as it manipulates DOM directly, bypassing React completely). 

Besides, currently `next/script` component is not concurrent rendering safe at all (see #40025), and calling concurrent API will cause the component (and its children) to opt-in concurrent rendering. If you add `startTransition` inside `next/script`, you may trigger many hidden race conditions.
author: SukkaW

body:
```suggestion
- The paths that have not been generated at build time will **not** result in a 404 page. Instead, Next.js will serve a [“fallback”](#fallback-pages) version of the page on the first request to such a path. Web crawlers, such as Google, won't be served a fallback and instead the path will behave as in [`fallback: 'blocking'`](#fallback-blocking).
- When a page with `fallback: true` is navigated to through `next/link` or `next/router` (client-side) Next.js will _not_ serve a fallback and instead the page will behave as [`fallback: 'blocking'`](#fallback-blocking).
```

body:
```suggestion
2. Can include named parameters: `/about/:path` matches `/about/a` and `/about/b` but not `/about/a/c`
3. Can have modifiers on named parameters (starting with `:`): `/about/:path*` matches `/about/a/b/c` because `*` is _zero or more_. `?` is _zero or one_ and `+` _one or more_
4. Can use regular expression enclosed in parenthesis: `/about/(.*)` is the same as `/about/:path*`
```

We should expose a configuration (like `experimental.useUndiciForNodeRuntimeFetch`) along side an environment variable.

Also, note that `undici.fetch`, `undici.Header`, `undici.Request` and `undici.Response` is only available since Node.js 16.8.0 (https://github.com/nodejs/undici/blob/33934f8f888ec0c03643c78c130bb9c7c43f1c19/index.js#L95). `undici` simply doesn't export these fields on Node.js < 16.8.0.

The best strategy for the client-specific RE would be to eliminate the bots from the list that
- don't match `/bot/`

body:
This is going to lead to more headaches than expected. CSS allows `\` to escape unicode chars without a `u` prefix, eg `"\2022"` should render a `•`, and `"\0025a0"` should render a `■`. Both of these are illegal escape sequences (they're legacy octal escapes in JS), but perfectly fine in CSS.

Rather than use JS's cooking semantics, we can manually cook the raw strings using CSS semantics. We can pilfer SWC's escape parser to do this: https://github.com/swc-project/swc/blob/decebadff1686adb8ea30d0ef84d849061205c7c/crates/swc_css_parser/src/lexer/mod.rs#L892-L969

body:
The wording here should be changed.

It is **React that does not** tolerate having a `div` inside `p`. **Because having a `div` inside `p` has violated the HTML5 spec**. Although the browser might tolerate it, React will throw an error to maintain a consistent result across browsers and avoid undefined behavior. It has nothing to do with Next.js.

Sites that load fonts with `font-display: swap` usually suffer from ([CLS](https://web.dev/cls/)) when the web font loads and replaces the fallback font. body:
`preconnect` is supported since Chrome 46, Edge 79, Safari 11.1, and Opera 33.3, which is good enough. Since IE 10-11 and Edge 12-18 are deprecated, we don't need to add extra `dns-prefetch` just for those deprecated browsers. As for Firefox, neither `preconnect` nor `dns-prefetch` is supported.

In short, adding extra `dns-prefetch` only results in bigger HTML, which is actually a negative optimization.

body:
**sugestion:** replace with `/runtime|getStaticProps|getServerSideProps|export const config/`

The reason why we had `matcher` and `unstable_allowDynamic` was to catch files with user config. But your idea of checking directly the presence of `export const config` is better than checking individual property names.

We can ignore `export let config` and `export var config` because the function `extractExportedConstValue()` ([here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/build/analysis/extract-const-value.ts#L219)) ignores them on purpose.

I remember discussing this with @javivelasco, and we agreed that it is preferable to prevent users having a non-const exported key.

body:
According to the WICG Priority Hints draft:

> With in-viewport images, a browser may automatically detect if the image is important and boost priority if it gets to layout early enough.

As the draft described, the browser could have detected the in-view image and automatically boost the fetch priority, it is probably unnecessary to dynamically set the `fetchpriority`.

Instead of dynamically setting `fetchpriority` to `high` when intersected and removing the `fetchpriority` when out of the viewport, personally I'd prefer setting a static `fetchpriority`.

`next/image` component already accepts the `priority={boolean}` prop. Would you like to change the implementation so that the `fetchpriority="high"` is set when `priority={true}` is specified?
author: SukkaW

body:
> Would you like to change the implementation so that the fetchpriority="high" is set when priority={true} is specified

Let's not do that without measuring the impact on performance. The last I checked, the `fetchpriority` prop is not as good as the current `priority` preloading prop.

body:
Query string values should likely take precedence over params.

I think the original RFC was hinting at that `query` was supposed to be the query string (URL search params) only, anyway: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/9524#:~:text=query%20%2D%20The%20query%20string%20(not%20entirely%20sure%20about%20this%20one%2C%20but%20probably%20needed)

Our docs says that `query` is:
> An object representing the query string, including dynamic route parameters.

But does not clarify if the query string value would be prioritized over the dynamic param value if they have the same key (ie. given `[id].js` and `/foo?id=bar`, should `query` resolve to `{id: "bar"}` instead of `{id: "foo"}`)? IMO it should. To get the dynamic param values, there is already `params`.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-server-side-props#context-parameter
author: balazsorban44

body: The dynamic route params are meant to take priority over query params so would be a breaking change to modify this.

Nevertheless Next.js is lax when it comes to JSON imports. So the fix in 0135a730d0c6cd399357941bbf0e68d5c373fe59 should work for now.

I guess this issue will bubble up as soon as Next.js will enforce ESM Type assertions. 

[More Info](https://github.com/eslint/eslint/discussions/15305)

author: SukkaW

body:
I think this should be reverted/use `module.exports` everywhere. See the reasoning here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/38647#discussion_r956475755

But basically, ESLint does not like `exports.default` (that SWC puts out)

I did try it earlier, but got an error from GitHub:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18369201/193256758-d3d6fa98-dc44-496d-b641-0f457b90ff91.png)

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/38647/commits/7781e7588c27468dc4d47817a01ecf594f869e47
author: balazsorban44

body:
But I **am not** using `export default` here.

I am using **a special syntax** `export =`: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/modules.html#export--and-import--require. In short, `export =` is how typescript tries to mimic `module.exports` behavior when not using `esModuleInterop`.

swc will transpile `export =` into `module.exports =` as well, [check this out on swc playground](https://play.swc.rs/?version=1.3.3&code=H4sIAAAAAAAAA0vOzysuUSgqzUlVsFWorrXmSq0oyC8qAXJAYtZcAMQs3l8gAAAA&config=H4sIAAAAAAAAA0WOQQrDQAhF7%2BI6i7bQTe%2FQQ8jUhAmZcVAHGkLuXg0J3an%2F%2BXSDWRO8NmgoShKVrtXwCy%2BwtZEmyc1gAFMfjbgo7d4IVh1ZSvBCmOwoerVcyDexGxe0nGAPGmUiJ4D0cbs%2FXbYwK526AUqueVzDkLg0IdV%2FhHVaLjJcF3vmWd%2F86UGY9ODPbjue95NuLFxn9Ud%2B40J6UesAAAA%3D)

body:
```suggestion
> **Note:** If you are incrementally migrating to a dynamic route (e.g. `[slug].js`) and using `fallback` for your `rewrites`, ensure you consider the case where pages are not found. By default, Next.js will match the dynamic route and end the routing phase. Using `notFound: true` in `getStaticProps` would return the `404.js` page content, or the default 404 page. If this is not desired, you can use `getServerSideProps` with `stale-while-revalidate` Cache-Control headers instead of `notFound: true`. Then, you can _manually_ proxy your existing backend using something like [http-proxy](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/38839#discussioncomment-3744442).
```

I think this could be even better
author: leerob

body:
```suggestion
> **Note:** If you are incrementally migrating to a dynamic route (e.g. `[slug].js`) and using `fallback: true` or `fallback: 'blocking'` along with a fallback `rewrite`, ensure you consider the case where pages are not found. When Next.js matches the dynamic route it stops checking any further routes. Using `notFound: true` in `getStaticProps` will return the 404 page without applying the fallback `rewrite`. If this is not desired, you can use `getServerSideProps` with `stale-while-revalidate` Cache-Control headers when returning your props. Then, you can _manually_ proxy to your existing backend using something like [http-proxy](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/38839#discussioncomment-3744442) instead of returning `notFound: true`.
```

author: feugy

body:
It would be useful but I'm not inclined to support it in this PR because:

- It's not obvious what it does: does it *add* headers to existing ones or does it *replace* all existing headers with the object?
- Ambiguity of headers: what if, for example, the user specify `{ Cookie: 'a=b', cookie: 'c=d' }`?

`Headers` nicely prevents these traps. I think *adding* new headers by specifying an object here is convenient but I'd hear feedbacks to be sure 😄
author: nuta

body:
For `json()`, yes. We don't allow returning a response in Next.js middleware so adding this feature to `json()` is meaningless and confusing.

Secondly, we (web servers) can't specify request headers in redirections (`redirect`) because browsers make another request, while `next()` and `rewrite()` are handled internally in server-side.

actually you can improve a bit here, you dont need to traverse all the params, just find the first wrong one (since the error does not specify how many or which params are wrong)

change to: 
``` javascript
(repeat && Array.isArray(paramValue) &&
paramValue.some((param) => typeof param !== 'string')) || ...
```

e.g. here stops at NaN, and you avoid going further which makes no sense at this point.

``` javascript
const paramValue = ['string 1', 'string 2', NaN, 'string 3', undefined, 8]
    
if(Array.isArray(paramValue) &&
 paramValue.some((param) => typeof param !== 'string')) {
  console.log('no valid')
 } else {
  console.log('valid')
 }



```

body:
```suggestion
Any `<Link />` in the viewport (initially or through scroll) will be prefetched by default (including the corresponding data) for pages using [Static Generation](/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props.md). The corresponding data for [server-rendered](/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props.md) routes is fetched _only when_ the <Link /> is clicked.
```
It's not prefetching if the link has already been clicked it is just fetching the data as needed for the transition since it is actually in progress.

body:
The point of this is to inform the user that errors are being ignored.

Currently a user of NextJS has no way of knowing that “Checking validity of types” does not check the validity of all types. It’s surprising to the user,  when running `tsc`, to find it produces different results than `next build`.

For example, NextJS currently warns when it modifies the users tsconfig, but not when it changes the results or exit code. This is unexpected.

Solutions can come in many forms
- Updating documentation (best paired with an option below)
- Updating existing “Checking validity of types” logs
- Add a warning (what I chose)
- Stop ignoring type errors


The idea is that through a change like one of the above, developers will know that some type errors are being ignored. If you dislike the messaging, perhaps it would be more clear if I mentioned that the user can run tsc for more information, or we could print the test file diagnostics as a warning as well.

Did that clarify things?

> should we rather use _document.js? Something like this

CSS imports doesn't work in `_document`, so not `@next/font` as well. See https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/41849#discussioncomment-3974155

> does the default family of any text become Inter?

Yeah it basically adds a class to the html tag like this:
```tsx
<html className="the-classname-from-inter-call">
```
```css
.the-classname-from-inter-call {
  font-family: "Inter", "Generated Inter Fallback";
}
```

author: alexkirsz

body:
Are we sure we don't want to trigger another pending refresh state if a new update came in while we were updating? A new update might clear up the errors that happened _during_ the previous update. Furthermore, in the exit condition, `onBuildOk` will be called **twice** instead of just once, since we will also call in the `else` branch below. I believe this logic is incorrect and we should do the following instead:

```suggestion
      tryApplyUpdates(
        onBeforeHotUpdate,
        onHotUpdateSuccess
      )
```

In which case we no longer need to pass these two functions as arguments to `tryApplyUpdates`, we can inline them directly instead.

body:
```suggestion
It's common to use [Middleware](/docs/advanced-features/middleware) for authentication or other purposes that involve rewriting the user to a different page. In order for the `<Link />` component to properly prefetch links with rewrites via Middleware, you need to tell Next.js both the URL to display and the URL to prefetch. This is required to avoid un-necessary fetches to middleware to know the correct route to prefetch. 
```

body:
I looked into getting rid of it, but unfortunately [`URLSearchParams` doesn't support nested objects](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/URLSearchParams/URLSearchParams#parameters), so I believe in this case `qs` is necessary.

`react-instantsearch` passes nested objects and arrays, which `URLSearchParams` can't convert. So we can't easily stringify/parse nested data structures without `qs` or some [Leetcode Hard-style solution](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56173848/want-to-convert-a-nested-object-to-query-parameter-for-attaching-to-url#answer-65831379).

body:
I'm not sure if this is an intended or unintended consequence, but this causes issues with CDNs that are configured to cache the routes `https://nextjs.org/_next/data/...`

With this change, the server would return an empty body `{}` for requests with the `x-middleware-prefetch` header which may be incidentally cached by the CDN.

For CDNs that do not allow configuring cache keys by custom headers such as Cloudflare, this empty body will be cached.

And if the user will then try to navigate to the "prefetched page", the Next.js application will throw an exception because it is then trying to load the same `https://nextjs.org/_next/data/...` URL without the `x-middleware-prefetch` header expecting the server-side props to be returned, but instead gets back `{}`.

body:
I think we don't need a new section for this, just a small note that e2e tests currently require `yarn` to be installed globally. No need to mention Corepack.

We could rather mention how e2e tests are run, something like:

> End-to-end (e2e) tests are run in complete isolation from the repository. When you [run](https://github.com/JanKaifer/next.js/blob/contributing-docs-yarn/contributing/core/testing.md#running-tests) an e2e test, a local version of `next` will be created inside your system's temp folder (eg. `/tmp`), which is then linked to the app, also created inside a temp folder. A server is started on a random port, against which the tests will run. After all tests have finished, the server is destroyed and all remaining files are deleted from the temp folder.

@styfle does this sound good?
author: balazsorban44

body:
Argh, my GitHub notifications still don't work.

But documenting how e2e works is definitely a good idea :+1:
author: jankaifer

body:
Ideally we would remove the need for yarn and use pnpm everywhere. 

The reason there is an issue here is that corepack enforces a single package manager for the entire repo. So it thinks its helping when its actually hurting lol. 

There is a new env var we can recommend to turn off this strict check called COREPACK_ENABLE_STRICT=0 that is available since corepack [0.15.0](https://github.com/nodejs/corepack/pull/197).

I think we should recommend that env var and also look into removing swapping all usages of yarn to pnpm.

body: This seems to be handled incorrectly as you can navigate from a page with `forceStatic` `true` to a page that is dynamic, or has `forceStatic` `false`. In that case searchParams will never be provided even though it should be provided. This would also mean that `forceStatic` has to be returned as part of the RSC payload when client-side navigating in order to make it consistent, however I think in those case we want searchParams to be available client-side.

body:
It’s unfortunate that it would scroll somewhere even if we don’t have to. Like a few pixels sometimes to reveal something that’s already there.

One thing we could do is use an intersectionobserver to observe the element and see if it’s sufficiently in the viewport and it not, then scrollIntoView.

The extra gap would also ensure that we wait for all other reflow before scrolling.
author: sebmarkbage

body:
Oh, completely forgot about `IntersectionObserver` that will even handle all the edge-cases correctly (like nested containers). Thanks for pointing that out.

But I'm a little confused by
> It’s unfortunate that it would scroll somewhere even if we don’t have to.

Because the current implementation shouldn't scroll if it's not needed. It will scroll only if the top of the page is not visible. The only controversial part is that it might scroll to the top and back (instantly) but it should have always the correct end result.

>The extra gap would also ensure that we wait for all other reflow before scrolling.

I'm not sure if I get this. Are you talking about the time gap when we are waiting for information from the `IntersectionObserver`?
author: jankaifer

body:
`IntersectionObserver` is too slow. It takes more than one frame so it will flicker. (It will show one frame unscrolled, one frame scrolled to the top and the next frame will be correct):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12820484/208431800-c93acc09-0656-4692-91ed-3e0f15dac245.png)
So it flickers even without scrolling to the top.

Let me check the previous implementation but I think that it was not an issue before.
author: jankaifer

body:
The old implementation is synchronous and does all the scrolling inside `componentDidMount`. It is way faster than `IntersectionObserver`. I'll look if I can make IntersectionObserver fast enough to fit into a single frame.

I also optimized the initial implementation so it causes just 3 reflows now.
author: jankaifer

body:
I haven't read the whole thread but it seems that `IntersectionObserver` is designed in a way that makes it impossible for us to use it synchronously.

If I understood correctly this thread: https://github.com/w3c/IntersectionObserver/issues/133
Then It will fire after the new content is on the screen which is exactly what we don't want.
author: jankaifer

body:
Synchronous != Fast. Synchronous means that if you do more than one thing, you do it more than once. For example, in this case cause reflows more than once. So synchronous means slower. (Same thing in React. Transitions can be faster than synchronous.)

IntersectionObserver should fire before the next rAF so it should be async but before painting. If that's not happening it might be because the React render itself is too late but that since odd since React itself doesn't render a transition during these events. So seems like something is off.
author: sebmarkbage

body:
:+1: Sorry I worded that poorly.
I know nothing about the internals of `IntersectionObserver` but I understood that it will generate new entries **after** each rAF: https://github.com/w3c/IntersectionObserver/issues/133#issuecomment-221690658

It shouldn't be a problem caused by react being late since I create a new `IntersectionObserver` for each scroll and even the second one is slow. Ideally, I want just one intersection observer, but it still shouldn't wait for the frame to be rendered each time.

I'll quickly check the specification at https://w3c.github.io/IntersectionObserver
EDIT: I haven't found anything interesting there.
author: jankaifer

author: sebmarkbage

body:
The rAF callback isn't what's interesting. It's whether or not the browser paints a new screen before scrolling. rAFs can also be tricky because they might not start immediately.

You also shouldn't be using promises or async/await for this, those just mess with the timing of things and get you put on the wrong queues in sequencing.

author: jankaifer

body:
I'm confused about why you're calling `requestAnimationFrame` at all here: https://github.com/JanKaifer/next.js/blob/13654cbda9076d7f182ced66c3f12f1aad3efe1b/packages/next/client/components/layout-router.tsx#L118

Intersection observers fire toward the end of the current frame and scheduling a rAF from within those would then end up in the next frame which is too late.

So seems you should just not call rAF and use the entries you get int he Intersection observer callback.
author: sebmarkbage

body: Btw, none of this should be done when we're navigating back/forward within a popstate, because there we should be able to rely on native scroll restoration (but we probably can't atm because React has a bug that it doesn't flush early enough).
